sections.name: Email Body
sections.content:
Subject: If the 3+3 is working → more pairs add rotation
Preheader: Three moms, three different kids, one common next step
Whether your child is 3 or 10, verbal or non-verbal, a few weeks into this or years into trying, if you're seeing any sign of awareness, you're past the hardest part.
The question now isn't "will it work?" It's "do I have enough pairs to make it consistent?"
Three parents, three different paths, one shared next step:
> *"He went from 'almost trained' to 'mostly there' in about 4 weeks. But with only 2 pairs, were doing laundry every night. The 3+3 changed everything."* 
>, L1 mom, verbal 4-year-old
> *"We saw the first pause in week 3. By week 5, she was initiating once a week. The BCBA said the consistency from having enough pairs in rotation made the difference."* 
>, L2 mom, sensory 6-year-old
> *"He's not trained. But he's feeling something for the first time in 8 years. That's enough for now. We added more pairs so he can wear them longer without me rushing to wash."* 
>, L3 mom, non-verbal 8-year-old
If any of those sound familiar, here's what they all share: the body-signal learning works best when the underwear is available every single day. Not washed overnight. Not "saved for later." Worn, consistently, day after day.
Here's why rotation matters, neurologically:
The body signal doesn't wire itself in a week. It takes repeated exposure, the 30-60 second "uh-oh" sensation, over and over, for the brain to learn: *this feeling means I need the bathroom.*
Every day without the signal is a day the neural pathway isn't firing.
Pull-ups wick the signal away. One pair in the wash means half a day without practice. The math is simple: more pairs = more consistent signal exposure = faster neural wiring.
Some kids show first signals in 2 weeks. Others need 8 weeks. Both are normal. The common thread: the underwear needs to be there, every day, for the learning to stick.
What to add based on where you started:
- If you started with 1 pair → The 3+3 Bundle gives you 6 pairs, 3 in rotation, 3 in the wash. Enough to never miss a day. Most parents tell us 1 pair showed them it works. 6 pairs made it stick.
- If you started with 3+3 → The 5+5 Bundle adds more rotation plus the Wash Bag, a waterproof travel pouch for the diaper bag, school, grandma's house. Because progress doesn't pause when you leave the house.
- If you started with 5+5 → Extra pairs and accessories keep the momentum going. The Magic Targets make bathroom visits engaging without pressure, a visual cue that turns aim into a game, for kids who respond to structure.
60-Day Guarantee, 60 days to try, full refund if it doesn't work for your child. Your judgment.
See what fits your rotation →
[Button: Find my next pair count]
*That's $13.33 per pair with the bundle — less than half the single-pair price.*
*60-day guarantee on every bundle. Add alongside your current routine. No replacement of anything. Just an addition.*
A few things parents ask at this point:
*"What if the 1 pair worked but I'm not sure about committing to more?"*
That's fair. The 60-day guarantee covers the upgrade too. If the 3+3 doesn't improve consistency, full refund on the whole bundle.
*"My child still resists wearing them sometimes."*
Some kids take to the fabric immediately. Others need 30 minutes of wear time, building up. That's normal. More pairs mean less pressure, you can wash and rotate without rushing.
*"Will more pairs actually speed things up?"*
The signal fires when the underwear is worn. More worn pairs = more firing opportunities. It's not magic. It's repetition. The nervous system learns through exposure, not intensity.
Whatever you decide, you're already doing what matters. You noticed something shifting. You're considering the next step. That's enough.
If thisn't the day to add more pairs, that's a real answer. The door is open.
I have two autistic sons. One trained at 6. The other at almost 8. I know what it's like to wonder if you're doing enough. You are.
Next time: Your final check-in — and what happens after Day 42.
With care,
Lena
,
Customer Support · Mom of two autistic sons
